title: Go Tell It on the Mountain
description: Baldwin's first novel follows fourteen-year-old John Grimes through a single day in 1930s Harlem, tracing family memory across generations shaped by migration, storefront faith, and Jim Crow constraint. A Radcliffe Top 100 classic on ALA's frequently challenged list.

title: The Fire Next Time
description: Two linked essays—letters on the centennial of Emancipation—examine Harlem childhood, Christian and Nation of Islam politics, and the everyday cost of American racism. Baldwin wrote for a nation still debating civil rights in real time.

citation.label: On the Anniversary of James Baldwin's 'The Fire Next Time' — Bucks County Beacon

title: Their Eyes Were Watching God
description: Janie Crawford's search for voice unfolds across three marriages and a Florida hurricane, rendered in Hurston's vernacular prose. Recovered by Black women scholars in the 1970s, the novel is now a staple of American literature curricula.

title: Black Boy
description: Wright's memoir records hunger, family violence, and everyday terror in the Jim Crow South, then his migration to Chicago and turn toward writing. The book documents how segregation structured childhood itself—not as abstraction, but as lived constraint.

title: Song of Solomon
description: Milkman Dead's search for family inheritance moves from Michigan to Pennsylvania and Virginia, blending folktale, flight legends, and the afterlife of slavery's property logic. Morrison's third novel won the National Book Critics Circle Award.

title: Sula
description: In a Black Ohio hillside community called the Bottom, Sula Peace and Nel Wright's lifelong bond fractures under grief, desire, and town judgment. Morrison's second novel tracks how a community defines and punishes difference.

title: Fallen Angels
description: Richie Perry, a Harlem teenager, lands in Vietnam after his college plans collapse. Myers documents combat's randomness, boredom between firefights, and the racial tensions inside a segregated-era military. Winner of the Coretta Scott King Award.

title: Hoops
description: Lonnie Jackson, a Harlem high-school basketball standout, navigates a city summer league where coaches, scouts, and gamblers press on his future. Myers writes the pressures on Black youth with the same documentary eye he brought to war and prison.

title: Monster
description: Sixteen-year-old Steve Harmon records his felony murder trial as a screenplay from a Manhattan detention cell, questioning how the state sees young Black men. Myers won the Michael L. Printz Award and a Coretta Scott King honor for the novel.

title: Kindred
description: Eda, a Black writer in 1970s California, is pulled backward to a Maryland plantation whenever her white ancestor's life is in danger. Butler uses speculative fiction to make slavery's violence immediate rather than distant textbook history.

title: The Autobiography of Malcolm X
description: Dictated to Alex Haley before Malcolm X's assassination, the memoir traces Lansing childhood, Boston and Harlem street life, prison conversion, Nation of Islam leadership, and the break toward independent Black politics.

title: Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry
description: Cassie Logan and her Mississippi family hold their land during the Depression as night riders, boycotts, and school segregation close in. Taylor drew on family stories; the novel won the 1977 Newbery Medal.

title: In Love and Trouble: Stories of Black Women
description: Thirteen stories—including "Roselily" and "Am I Blue?"—trace rural and urban Black women's desire, grief, and resistance across the South. Walker published the collection three years before The Color Purple.

title: The Best Short Stories by Black Writers
description: Hughes edited this landmark anthology—first published as The Best Short Stories by Negro Writers—collecting work from 1899 to 1967 by writers including Baldwin, Hurston, and Wright. It remains a syllabus touchstone for short-form Black literary history.
